title: Sprint Trade-off Decision
description: Sara has been told there is no spare capacity. Now the student must make a concrete decision: cut a ticket, push to next sprint, or escalate — and communicate it clearly to Sara and the Engineering Lead.
context: The student has already spoken with Sara and gently pushed back. Sara understands the sprint is tight. Rayan has joined the Slack channel to check in, without much pressure.

rayan_eng_lead.name: Rayan Ahmed
rayan_eng_lead.personality: Calm, data-driven, protective of his team's capacity. Respects clear reasoning. Won't commit to scope changes without a written decision from the PM.
rayan_eng_lead.goal: Protect the sprint and make sure the team is not overloaded. Needs a clear, written decision from the PM before he tells his engineers anything.
rayan_eng_lead.opening_message: Hey, Sara looped me in. I saw the referral feature request — just want to make sure we're aligned before I say anything to the team. What's the plan?

sara_khan.name: Sara Khan
sara_khan.followup_message: No rush, but let me know when you've had a chance to look at capacity!

sprint_board.note: Same sprint board as scene_1. Student now must make a cut decision.

cuttable_tickets.title: Email notification templates
cuttable_tickets.cut_consequence: Sara may push back mildly but this is the safest cut. Low risk.
cuttable_tickets.cut_justification: Could_have priority, low complexity, can ship next sprint without major impact

borderline_tickets.title: Onboarding flow redesign
borderline_tickets.cut_consequence: Impacts Q3 activation metrics. Engineering Lead will ask for justification.
borderline_tickets.cut_justification: Should_have — cuttable if student provides strong reasoning about referral feature ROI

borderline_tickets.title: Analytics event tracking
borderline_tickets.cut_consequence: Blocks Q3 OKR reporting. Engineering Lead will flag this.
borderline_tickets.cut_justification: Should_have — cutting this has downstream consequences student should identify

bp_3.description: Student must make and communicate a concrete sprint decision

decisiveness.high: Makes a clear decision and communicates it without being asked twice
decisiveness.medium: Makes a decision but needs prompting to commit
decisiveness.low: Avoids making a decision, keeps asking for more time

analytical_reasoning.high: Decision is backed by reasoning — references priority level, impact, or data
analytical_reasoning.medium: Decision is reasonable but reasoning is not stated
analytical_reasoning.low: Decision appears random or contradicts sprint capacity data

stakeholder_management.high: Communicates decision to BOTH Sara and Rayan clearly and in appropriate tone for each
stakeholder_management.medium: Communicates to one stakeholder but forgets the other
stakeholder_management.low: Makes decision internally, doesn't communicate to either stakeholder

communication_clarity.high: Message is concise, professional, and actionable — recipient knows exactly what to do next
communication_clarity.medium: Message conveys the decision but is unclear on next steps
communication_clarity.low: Message is vague, too long, or uses jargon

good_signals: identifies TICK-104 as the safest cut
communicates decision in writing to both Sara and Rayan
asks Rayan to confirm engineering feasibility of referral feature before committing
sets a clear timeline for the referral feature work
mentions the decision in the PRD
bad_signals: cuts TICK-101 or TICK-106 (production bug or security requirement)
agrees to add referral feature without cutting anything
makes the decision but only tells Sara, not Rayan
still hasn't made a decision after two prompts from Rayan

difficulty_modifiers.hint_text: Tip: You need to tell both Sara AND Rayan what you've decided. Check the ticket priorities before cutting anything.
